text:
Purpose:
Quick working notes for shipping a fast backfill/handoff flow to get legacy unlabeled data into the new adjudication queue. This is a scratchpad — ideas, tiny scripts, and TODOs. Not final.

Context:
- We have ~1.2M legacy rows with partial metadata (session_id, raw_text, source).
- Existing labeling UI expects canonical_id and normalized text; legacy rows frequently miss canonical_id and contain noisy punctuation, XML fragments, or truncated sentences.
- Need a low-friction backfill that: (1) dedupes obvious duplicates, (2) canonicalizes IDs where possible, (3) surfaces a small, high-signal subset for human adjudication, and (4) routes remainder to a cheaper microtask path.

Goals (metrics to aim for):
- Human adjudication queue: 10–15k highest-uncertainty items per week (manageable throughput for two contractors).
- Automated/cheap microtasks: 60–75% of legacy pool after si...

Meeting: 1:1 prep with Procurement lead (Lina) — purpose: reconcile open capex commitments vs approved spend, clear odd approvals, align on near-term PO cadence.

Context notes (tl;dr):

- A handful of vendor invoices are in flight but missing SOW sign-off (see FIN-2789).
- Dedicated infra appliance purchase (EdgeCluster-3) has flexible ship date but sticky vendor cancellation fee ($45k) if we delay past 2025-12-01.

Agenda (30 min):

2) Items to question for deferral/cut (10m) — list candidate POs + risk note.
3) Procurement approval queue sanity check (5m) — any >$100k stuck on legal/KMS?

title: Anchored session credit stitchpad
preview:
Purpose:
Quick personal scratchpad for an anchored session-credit model that blends PAYG token metering with light session commitments. Aim: reduce perceived billing friction for interactive apps (chat UX) while preserving unit-econ clarity and limiting subsidy exposure.

Context / motivation:
- Lots of product team...
text:
Purpose:
Quick personal scratchpad for an anchored session-credit model that blends PAYG token metering with light session commitments. Aim: reduce perceived billing friction for interactive apps (chat UX) while preserving unit-econ clarity and limiting subsidy exposure.

Context / motivation:
- Lots of product teams want a simple UX: "buy session credits" rather than thinking about tokens. But we need to map credits back to tokens and GPU seconds to understand cost.
- Want a middle ground between flat subs and pure payg: small upfront anchors (cheap) that cover a guaranteed baseline of concurrency and warm KV caches, plus per-token taper after anchor is consumed.

Key primitives (names I use here):
- Anchor: upfront small credit (micro-commit) purchased on first significant use. Covers warmups, short prefixes, and a buffer for bursts.
- Session credit: unit consumed per user session ...

text:
Objective:
Short session notes capturing commands tried, raw snippets, and working hypotheses for the intermittent throughput oscillations observed on node pool-rt-3 when continuous_batching is enabled. Goal is to preserve breadcrumbs for follow-up and handoff to SRE/infra.

Summary of symptom:
- Starting 2025-10-29 we saw periodic ~40-70% drop in tokens/s for baseline text-generation workloads (seq_len=512, concurrency=40). Drops last 2–6 minutes then recover. Not correlated with GPU utilization spikes (GPU stays ~60–75%).
- Regression appears only when continuous_batching=true and kv_prefetch=true on the same runtime revision (serving-runtime:v2.14.1). No regression when we disable continuous_batching or pin to previous runtime v2.13.8.

Quick reproducer (local-ish):
1) Launch small cluster: kubectl apply -f test-cluster.yaml (k8s job using model-opt-7b)
2) Load generator: locust or...

title: Cadence-based prompt sandbox
preview:
Purpose:
This is a personal sandbox to explore the idea of 'cadence' in prompting — i.e., the rhythm and timing of instructions, few-shot injections, and turn-level scaffolds across a multi-turn or streaming interaction. Goal is practical: identify brittle cases where changing the tempo (pace, density of examples, d...
text:
Purpose:
This is a personal sandbox to explore the idea of 'cadence' in prompting — i.e., the rhythm and timing of instructions, few-shot injections, and turn-level scaffolds across a multi-turn or streaming interaction. Goal is practical: identify brittle cases where changing the tempo (pace, density of examples, distance between examples and query) materially changes outputs and to capture simple mitigation patterns.

Quick hypothesis notes (working):
- H1: Denser few-shot injections immediately before a query increase stylistic conformity but can reduce creativity and cause token inflation.
- H2: Interleaving short clarifying instructions between examples (mini-prompts) improves factual recall on long-context tasks by anchoring the model's attention.
- H3: A fixed cadence (e.g., example -> 2-line instruction -> example -> question) yields more consistent output distribution than ad...

text:
Quick context:
- Working notes for a set of small, targeted refactors that came out of PR #4876 review (routing + KV-cache edge-case fixes). Not a grand design doc, more of a map + prioritized TODOs for next few sprints.
- Goal: reduce cognitive overhead in request routing, make fallback logic explicit, and remove two hotspots in request path that are repeatedly causing timeouts for long-seq workloads.
- Constraints: minimal API surface change, keep canary rolloutable, preserve current telemetry hooks.

High-level summary (one-liner):
- Break routing logic into three deterministic layers: policy resolution, model-compatibility, and fallback orchestration. Move heuristics out of hot path and into a small cache-backed decision service.

Why this matters (observed pain):
- Multiple incidents where fallback routing reselected a larger model causing latency spikes (seen in prod incidents 2...

title: Tokenization Lattice + Mapper — scratchpad
preview:
Purpose:
This is a personal scratchpad exploring an API shape and runtime behaviour for a tokenization lattice + mapper service. Goal: produce a lightweight contract that lets the client and multiple tokenizer implementations interoperate for streaming generation, partial offsets, alignment with original bytes, and ...
text:
Purpose:
This is a personal scratchpad exploring an API shape and runtime behaviour for a tokenization lattice + mapper service. Goal: produce a lightweight contract that lets the client and multiple tokenizer implementations interoperate for streaming generation, partial offsets, alignment with original bytes, and robust fallback when tokenizer variants disagree.

Background / motivation:
- We need consistent token -> byte/char offsets for: eval alignment, user-visible cursor highlighting, and incremental cache resumption.
- Different tokenizers (BPE, unigram, byte-level) used across model family variants and quantized runtime can disagree on token boundaries.
- In streaming scenarios, we often emit tokens before client has the whole completion; clients need to map tokens back to original input text reliably for highlighting and incremental diffing.

Design goals / constraints:
- Low...

text:
Quick capture from 2025-09-03 call + field testing notes. Rough, mostly for internal follow-up.

Context:
- Customer: Nightbloom Ware (on-prem VPC pilot). Heavy SKU catalog (300k SKUs). Using our hosted-private build but routing through their VPC for data isolation.
- Primary ask: conversational search + summary for product pages with strict latency SLO (p95 < 220ms) and deterministic cost.
- Customer contact: Jared M. (integration lead), ops: Lina K.

Call / field notes:
- Observed: high token counts in real user queries; many SKUs have long hyphenated identifiers and embedded micro-unicode (non-breaking hyphens, zero-width joiners). Tokenizer splits these unpredictably -> token blowup (example: SKU "NB-λ-004‑XZ" became 12 tokens).
- Customer logs show repeated re-tokenization across reruns; cache hit-rate ~8% (expected 40%). Seems normalization mismatch between preproc on their edge...

title: Adaptive system instruction overlays - scratchpad
preview:
Purpose / quick summary:

This is a working sketch for an "adaptive overlay" subsystem: a lightweight, versioned layer of system-instruction fragments ("overlays") that can be composed at inference time to provide app-, user-, or session-specific guidance to models without requiring full model fine-tuning or unsafe ...
text:
Purpose / quick summary:

This is a working sketch for an "adaptive overlay" subsystem: a lightweight, versioned layer of system-instruction fragments ("overlays") that can be composed at inference time to provide app-, user-, or session-specific guidance to models without requiring full model fine-tuning or unsafe ad-hoc system prompts.

Goals:
- Allow apps to attach small, structured instructions that modify behavior (tone, safety filters, domain constraints) at request time.
- Make overlays composable and precedence-ordered (global < app < user < session < ephemeral).
- Support partial patching (diffs) and rollbacks with lightweight versioning.
- Integrate with Redwood routing/caching so overlays are cache-key aware and don't blow up batching efficiency.
- Make it easy for platform teams to audit and enforce safety constraints on overlays.

High-level concepts:
- Overlay: small str...

Keep it lightweight — this is intentionally messy.

Summary of what I saw (sketchy):
- Window: 2025-02-15 -> 2025-02-24; uptick in short-response hallucinations on policy-related prompts. Rate roughly 3x baseline for 2-token injections.
- Affected model variants: rdx-small-v2 (quantized) and rdx-small-v2-fp16; rdx-medium unaffected.
- Symptoms: confident but incorrect factual asserts, truncated follow-ups, context-dropping mid-dialog.
- Quick repro notes: often when prompt contains a short directive + URL, output replaces URL text with a plausible-sounding domain description.

title: Cascade abstain protocol — draft notes
preview:
Purpose:

Short: sketch a lightweight, deployable protocol for when labelers and automated pipelines should return an explicit \"ABSTAIN\" label versus a best-effort label, and how to cascade those abstains into downstream routing (auto-adjudicate, human queue, escalate to SME, or accept fallback).

Context / motiva...
text:
Purpose:

Short: sketch a lightweight, deployable protocol for when labelers and automated pipelines should return an explicit \"ABSTAIN\" label versus a best-effort label, and how to cascade those abstains into downstream routing (auto-adjudicate, human queue, escalate to SME, or accept fallback).

Context / motivation:
- We see a consistent fraction (~2.6% of samples) flagged by passive heuristics as \"uncertain\" and another small tail where model confidence disagrees with annotator confidence. When we force a best-effort label we create noisy signal; when we over-abstain we bloat the adjudication queue. Need a middle path.
- Goal: reduce false labels in production-sensitive fields (PII redaction, SKU mapping) while keeping human cost bounded.
- This doc is intentionally UX-lite: focus on rules and simple routing, not UI design.

Design principles:
- Deterministic: rules should be ...

title: Contextual Instruction Perturbation Roadmap
preview:
OVERVIEW
This is a personal scratchpad / working roadmap for a set of experiments I'm running around 'instruction perturbation' and sparse conditioning strategies. Goal: find small, robust prompt mutation strategies that reduce hallucination and brittle instruction-following while staying within tight token budgets ...
text:
OVERVIEW
This is a personal scratchpad / working roadmap for a set of experiments I'm running around 'instruction perturbation' and sparse conditioning strategies. Goal: find small, robust prompt mutation strategies that reduce hallucination and brittle instruction-following while staying within tight token budgets for production calls.

Quick motivation notes (TL;DR):
- Many of our app prompts break when the user-provided context contains noise: fragments, list artifacts, or repeated instructions.
- Full prompt sanitation + heavy context engineering is expensive (tokens + dev time).
- Hypothesis: small, systematic perturbations to the instruction + a curated mini few-shot set can make models more robust to noisy context and adversarial fragments.

CURRENT HYPOTHESES
1) Sparse conditioning (1-2 targeted exemplars) + instruction anchor reduces hallucinations versus vanilla single-shot ...

title: Attenuation Policy Language (APL) — proposal
preview:
Purpose
---
Quick, messy proposal for an "Attenuation Policy Language" (APL) to express runtime attenuation rules: reduce, mask, or transform model outputs at the serving layer when outputs hit a safety/PII/contract surface. This is a personal scratchpad — not final.

Motivation
- We need a compact, reviewable way t...
text:
Purpose
---
Quick, messy proposal for an "Attenuation Policy Language" (APL) to express runtime attenuation rules: reduce, mask, or transform model outputs at the serving layer when outputs hit a safety/PII/contract surface. This is a personal scratchpad — not final.

Motivation
- We need a compact, reviewable way to express output-safety rules that can be evaluated in the runtime before tokens are emitted (or in a streaming-safe fencing point).
- Existing approaches are ad-hoc (per-model hooks, hardcoded regexes, or heavy NLP classifiers). APL aims to: be auditable, versionable, testable, and cheap to evaluate.
- Use cases: redact user PII, enforce contractual phrases, clamp hallucination risk for certain tenant tiers, or apply length/verbosity attenuation in low-cost routes.

Goals / non-goals
- Goals: declarative, composable attenuation primitives; static analysis for rollout; tele...

Quick context:
- BlueCrest = Series B insurtech (specializes in small-commercial property & casualty underwriting). ~220 employees, HQ Chicago.
- CTO: Anika Shah (technical buyer). Head of Innovation: Marcus Li (product / pilot sponsor). Procurement: likely later.
- Why now: they announced a claims triage automation initiative (press release Nov 2024) and are hiring ML infra/ML engineering roles. Good timing for pilot.

What we think they care about (hypothesis):
- Cost predictability for production inference (claims volume spikes during storms)
- Data isolation / compliance (PII in claims text, PHI in occasional medical invoices)
- Low-lift integration for product teams to show ROI quickly (customer-facing, underwriters + claims adjusters)
- Telemetry: need traces, per-route latency/cost to justify economics to CFO

text:
Purpose:
Quick personal cheat-sheet for resolving contradictory statements in labeling tasks (dialogue, user metadata, multi-field records). Meant as an aide for fast adjudication and creating examples for the labeling guide. Not canonical — propose changes to the shared guide after validating across samples.

Scope / common contexts seen in our datasets:
- Short multi-turn chats where the user later corrects themself ("No, I meant Tuesday not Wednesday").
- System/user metadata disagreements (profile says "NYC" but user message says "I'm in SF now").
- Multi-field documents where different fields state different entity values (shipping_address vs billing_address).
- Extractive QA pairs where source paragraphs contradict each other.
- OCR/ASR artifacts where recognition errors create contradictions.

Guiding principles (ordered):
1) Preserve original text: the label should reflect wha...

title: Auto-batcher heuristics debug
preview:
Scratchpad: auto-batcher behaviour investigation (misc notes + commands run)

Summary:
- Nightly regression: p50 token throughput dropped ~18% for chat endpoint between 2025-02-11 02:00 & 02:30 UTC. No model change. Traffic distribution shifted slightly (more short-turn prompts), but not enough to account for drop. ...
text:
Scratchpad: auto-batcher behaviour investigation (misc notes + commands run)

Summary:
- Nightly regression: p50 token throughput dropped ~18% for chat endpoint between 2025-02-11 02:00 & 02:30 UTC. No model change. Traffic distribution shifted slightly (more short-turn prompts), but not enough to account for drop. Hypothesis: continuous-batcher heuristic misfired (thresholds or temporal decay) causing smaller batches + KV-cache thrash.

What I saw in metrics (quick):
- baseline (2025-02-11 01:50): p50 tokens/sec ~ 3200, p95 latency 120ms
- regression window (02:05–02:25): p50 tokens/sec ~ 2600, p95 latency 160ms
- runtime CPU on hosts jumped ~12% during window, queue length increased modestly
- observed cache miss rate for prefix cache jumped from 4% -> 21% (sampling from /metrics/kvcache/miss_rate)

Commands I ran (local):
- tail recent logs (pod): kubectl -n serving-runtime logs -f...

text:
Purpose:
This is a compact, practical crib for the start-of-shift transition. Not a polished runbook — quick notes, recent context, and the first-pass mitigation steps I actually use. Keep it short, update live during the takeover.

Start-of-shift checklist (10–15 min):
- Check PagerDuty: acknowledge any unassigned incidents and skim active alerts.
- Slack: open #infra-ops and #prod-incidents. Note pinned messages and any OOO coverage calls.
- Deploy window: verify there are no scheduled rollouts in the next 2 hours (Confluence -> Deploy Calendar).
- Capacity: glance at Console -> Dedicated pools -> GPU util. If >75% sustained, flag for scale.
- Token costs: spot any sudden spikes in per-route token usage in the last 30m.
- Smoke test: run minimal inference path against /health and basic generation sample for main models.

Current active alerts (snapshot at handoff):
- Route timeout s...

preview:

text:
Context:
- Late-stage dev: small perf/regression spike observed in hosted text-generation v2 route (low-prompt-length).
- Customer report + internal smoke tests: occasional incoherent completions (~0.6% -> 1.9% of sample). Need fast triage before escalation.

Objective (5–20 minute prioritization):
- Rapidly determine if regression is model, routing, or pre/post-processing.
- Produce small reproducible sample set + ad-hoc rubric to categorize failure mode.

Quick hypothesis list (ordered cheap->expensive):
1) Prompt tokenization change (tokenizer update or subtle encoding mismatch).
2) Quantization/precision path change (new kernel selection for short sequences).
3) Routing fallback to smaller model variant under burst.
4) Prompt truncation or prefix drop due to cache bug.
5) Data corruption in the request body at edge (rare but check).

Ad-hoc rubric (for quick labeling):
- Category ...

title: API key heartbeat / window probe notes
preview:
Context / quick summary:
- Observed recurring 401/429 bursts for a subset of long-lived API keys (mostly internal test clients + some partner integrations). Pattern: relatively low sustained QPS, then periodic clustered 429 spikes every ~60s for a small cohort of keys. Suspect interaction between a "heartbeat" clien...
text:
Context / quick summary:

Suspect interaction between a "heartbeat" client behavior (periodic keepalive or metrics ping) and sliding window quota enforcement.

- Hypotheses for root cause

- Short-term mitigations to reduce customer impact

text:
Context:
Ridgewood Retail (mid-market omnichannel retailer) wants an in-store + web assistant that answers product availability, returns policy, and guided troubleshooting for electronics accessories. Primary ask: low-latency product Q&A with an offline fallthrough for kiosk mode. They’re sensitive to PII and customer order IDs — legal wants a private deployment or strict VPC/KMS controls.

High-level constraints / goals (customer stated):
- P95 latency target: <= 200ms for short Q&A (single-turn), P99 < 600ms for multi-step dialogs.
- Sustained peak traffic: 150–250 requests/sec (region: us-west). Weekend spikes up to 400rps for promotions.
- Cost target: keep per-conversation token cost under $0.08 on average (we need to propose model + quantization mix).
- Data control: VPC peering + KMS; no customer data stored in shared buckets.
- Rollout: start in web + api, then add in-store ki...

title: Optimistic Tool Invocation API — personal sketch
preview:
Quick personal sketch for an optimistic tool-invocation API pattern.

Context / motivation:
- Want a lightweight pattern where the model can request external tool calls (search, DB lookup, code-runner, calculator) but the caller (client or platform) doesn't have to block synchronous LLM response until the tool finis...
text:
Quick personal sketch for an optimistic tool-invocation API pattern.

Context / motivation:
- Want a lightweight pattern where the model can request external tool calls (search, DB lookup, code-runner, calculator) but the caller (client or platform) doesn't have to block synchronous LLM response until the tool finishes.
- Useful when: tool latency is variable and we prefer a fast optimistic reply + later patch/update; or client UX can stream a provisional answer and then incorporate factual corrections when the tool result arrives.
- Goal: surface an API that supports: optimistic (speculative) invocation, robust reconciliation, idempotent updates, auditing, cancellation, and simple developer ergonomics.

Constraints / design goals:
- Minimal server-side state for the hosted path; negotiation-friendly for private deployments.
- Default safe: optimistic mode must have explicit opt-in by...

title: Cache tunneling & cold-start runbook (scratch)
preview:
Purpose:
Quick personal runbook and scratchpad for exploring a lightweight "cache tunneling" strategy to reduce cold-start tail latency when a shared KV cache is cold across tenants. Not formal yet — scribbles, assumptions, and next steps.

Background / hypothesis:
- Problem: when a model instance loses its KV cache...
text:
Purpose:
Quick personal runbook and scratchpad for exploring a lightweight "cache tunneling" strategy to reduce cold-start tail latency when a shared KV cache is cold across tenants. Not formal yet — scribbles, assumptions, and next steps.

Background / hypothesis:
- Problem: when a model instance loses its KV cache (warmup evicted or new allocation), p95/p99 on the first ~20 requests spikes hugely (often 4–8x), hurting interactive experiences.
- Idea: proactively inject compact prefix priming across low-cost background requests (tunnels) that are batched and scheduled during low-util windows. These priming requests are cheap, synthetic, and target popular prefix keys to accelerate real traffic warmup.

Experiment setup (run 2025-01-16):
- Model: open-llama-13b-8k (quantized FP16-ish stack) on a single A100-equivalent instance.
- Workload generator: local script (./tools/arrival-sim) ...

title: Abbrev expansion & casemap: scratchpad
preview:
Context / why I care:
This is a personal scratchpad for handling abbreviation expansion and case-mapping edge cases in the user-text corpus. We get systematic errors downstream in the NER and intent models when abbreviations or mixed-case tokens are treated inconsistently between training labels, tokenizers, and pos...
text:
Context / why I care:
This is a personal scratchpad for handling abbreviation expansion and case-mapping edge cases in the user-text corpus. We get systematic errors downstream in the NER and intent models when abbreviations or mixed-case tokens are treated inconsistently between training labels, tokenizers, and postprocessing. Goal is to propose a small set of deterministic heuristics we can (a) run as a preprocessing pass for labeling, and (b) optionally apply at inference output postprocessing.

Current pain points observed (examples):
- 'u.s.' vs 'US' vs 'U.S.A' in location/entity fields -> sometimes labeled as GPE, sometimes O. Tokenizer splits 'U.S.' into ['U', '.', 'S', '.'] which breaks span alignment.
- 'Acme, Inc' vs 'Acme Inc.' vs 'ACME INC' -> company names normalized inconsistently, hurts dedupe and model recall.
- Measurements: '5kg' vs '5 kg' vs '5-kg' -> unit attached ...

Not a final playbook; mostly notes, quick sims, and checklist items to iterate with SRE and SEs.

- Which warmup shapes (linear, exponential, random jitter) best expose regressions early without creating false positives?
- Is our automatic fallback (tiered variant fallback) stable when regional capacity hits soft limits?

- Stochastic ramp: 0 -> 1% -> 3% -> 7% -> 15% -> 30...

title: Annotator mental models and fallback triggers
preview:
Notes-to-self: running through how annotators actually think when they see messy inputs. Goal: capture common mental shortcuts + explicit fallback triggers so we can codify into small decision rules and a short adjudication checklist. Useful for new hires + quick adjudication on ambiguous samples.

High-level observ...
text:
Notes-to-self: running through how annotators actually think when they see messy inputs. Goal: capture common mental shortcuts + explicit fallback triggers so we can codify into small decision rules and a short adjudication checklist. Useful for new hires + quick adjudication on ambiguous samples.

High-level observations:
- Annotators lean heavily on perceived intent rather than literal tokens when unsure (eg: 'Sent from my iPhone' -> usually signature).
- When text is noisy (OCR or chat logs), annotators prefer to preserve coherent semantic spans and ignore artefactual punctuation.
- People default to: if something reads like a social sign-off (thanks, cheers, best) + a name-like token -> label as SIGNATURE. If there is an email/phone under it -> reinforce signature.
- Quotes/replies: many annotate inline replies as NOT signature even if they contain a sign-off, because it's context...

title: CallPack credit arbitrage notes
preview:
Purpose:
Quick brain-dump on a small "callpack" construct that can be used as a compact credit unit for interactive apps. Focus: reduce friction for tiny/embedded apps, make burst pricing predictable, and close some arbitrage currently visible in sessionized credit schemes.

Context / motivation:
- Many micro-usage ...
text:
Purpose:
Quick brain-dump on a small "callpack" construct that can be used as a compact credit unit for interactive apps. Focus: reduce friction for tiny/embedded apps, make burst pricing predictable, and close some arbitrage currently visible in sessionized credit schemes.

Context / motivation:
- Many micro-usage customers hate per-token math. They want 'calls' or 'messages' that are easy to explain to non-technical PMs and finance.
- Existing credit schemes (tokens-per-credit) open arbitrage when customers craft very long prompts or extremely long completions. Also cache/prefix behavior complicates unit economics.
- Goal: design a unit (callpack) that maps to an expected compute cost envelope, supports prefix caching discounts, and is still intuitive.

High-level idea:
- 1 callpack ~= budgeted compute for 1 interactive turn with mild completion (eg 512-1024 total tokens traveling t...

title: Ephemeral policy consolidation — lab notes
preview:
Context / problem statement:
- We currently have several ephemeral policy surfaces scattered across client SDKs, ingress proxy, and runtime adapters. This causes surprising behavior during load spikes and rollout fiddling (e.g., token TTLs differ, prefetch gating inconsistent).
- Goal: consolidate ephemeral decision...
text:
Context / problem statement:
- We currently have several ephemeral policy surfaces scattered across client SDKs, ingress proxy, and runtime adapters. This causes surprising behavior during load spikes and rollout fiddling (e.g., token TTLs differ, prefetch gating inconsistent).
- Goal: consolidate ephemeral decision logic into a small, auditable policy layer that sits between ingress and routing so client SDKs can remain thin and runtime operators can tune a single source-of-truth.

Design goals (non-exhaustive):
- Single semantic model for short-lived policies (TTL, prewarm, crediting, retry-backoff, safelist overrides).
- Lightweight, human readable DSL with stable defaults.
- Support per-tenant overrides and per-route shims without duplicating whole policies.
- Plumb telemetry events for later retrospective analysis.
- Keep client SDK changes minimal: client sends a small context b...

title: adaptive ingress policy graph sketch
preview:
RATIONALE:
This is a lightweight sketch for an "adaptive ingress policy graph" that can express routing decisions across availability zones, model variants, dedicated pools, and cost/latency tradeoffs. The goal is a compact, composable DSL that: 
- is serializable (JSON/YAML),
- supports runtime evaluation with simp...
text:
RATIONALE:
This is a lightweight sketch for an "adaptive ingress policy graph" that can express routing decisions across availability zones, model variants, dedicated pools, and cost/latency tradeoffs. The goal is a compact, composable DSL that: 
- is serializable (JSON/YAML),
- supports runtime evaluation with simple predicates,
- can be compiled to rules used by the ingress router,
- enables incremental rollout / canary policies and deterministic fallbacks.

Key design goals (tradeoffs):
- Expressiveness vs evaluation speed: prefer simple boolean/enum predicates, numeric thresholds, and weighted edges rather than full Turing-complete expressions.
- Deterministic fallback: fallback path must be reproducible given request metadata + random seed.
- Observability hooks: nodes emit reason codes and metrics tags for tracing.
- Versioned semantics: policy JSON must include version + valida...

text:
Notes to self / lab log — aim: carve out small, high-leverage heuristics we can apply in preprocessing and UI to reduce labeler confusion. Keep this intentionally messy.

Context:
- We're seeing lots of micro-failures in adjudication for small classes: signature lines, inline timestamps, embedded code, and OCR hyphenation. These contribute to high disagreement and long adjudication windows.
- Goal: identify deterministic "firebreaks" (cheap transformations + labeler guidance) that remove noise without biasing ground truth.

Principles:
1) Favor transformations that are reversible or that only affect obviously non-semantic tokens (common credit-card masks, long UUIDs, boilerplate footers).
2) If ambiguous, show both raw + transformed in UI and record provenance flag.
3) Keep label granularity consistent: prefer token-level spans only when annotators agree >80% historically, otherwise l...

title: Compact HTTP/2 Session Multiplexing Labbook
preview: Purpose: personal labbook sketch exploring a compact, backpressure-aware HTTP/2-based session multiplexing scheme tuned for client-side proxies and constrained devices. This is a working brain-dump (not final). Aim: reduce per-stream header overhead, enable efficient preemption/reassembly, and define a small control...
text:
Purpose: personal labbook sketch exploring a compact, backpressure-aware HTTP/2-based session multiplexing scheme tuned for client-side proxies and constrained devices. This is a working brain-dump (not final). Aim: reduce per-stream header overhead, enable efficient preemption/reassembly, and define a small control API for client proxies to express intent to the runtime.

High-level goals:
- Keep wire semantics compatible with standard HTTP/2 stream multiplexing, but introduce a minimal, optional layer (encoded inside DATA/HEADERS) for compact session framing that supports: prioritized micro-streams, resumable partial payloads, and explicit backpressure signals.
- Avoid adding an entirely new transport when possible; target places where clients already speak HTTP/2 (browsers, mobile proxies, edge connectors).
- Provide a tight config surface so edge proxies can trade latency vs throu...

title: Acorn Utilities pilot onramp scratchpad

text:
Context: kickoff call with Acorn Utilities (smart meters + edge gateways). They want a private deployment behind their VPC for event enrichment and anomaly triage. Goal for pilot: ingest meter telemetry -> short prompt summarization + anomaly score -> push to ops queue. 30k events/day expected initially, bursty in morning windows.

Participants: Tom Harwood (Head of Data), Leila Gomez (Platform Eng), Ravi Patel (SRE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Daniel Kim (Redwood infra)

Raw notes:
- They are using AWS in us-east-1; current infra: EKS Fargate for ingestion, Kafka (MSK) for streaming, Postgres for metadata.
- Security: must use customer KMS for envelope keys; no data plane egress to public internet.
- Network: strict VPC whitelist, restricted subnets. They prefer VPC endpoint + Transit Gateway.
- Latency SLO: per-event inference < 700ms p95 (for triage path). Batch processing windo...
